coredata.dc:title: Green, fast and scalable preparation of few-layers graphene
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Quanzhu
Yuan, Guohui
Guo, Kejun
Li, Shaojun
Lin, Minjing
Hong, Jing
Huang, Youyuan
coredata.dc:description: Few-layers graphene is prepared by one-step liquid-phase exfoliation with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as exfoliating agents, which is prepared by a novel electrochemical interface technology. The morphology, structure and composition of CQDs and graphene, as well as the mechanism for the realization of large-scale preparation of CQDs and graphene was proposed. The results showed that CQDs with D50 size of 55.12 nm has excellent dispersion and exfoliation properties for graphite, and the graphene yield is as high as 97.25% (D50 size of 3.651 μm and 1–5 layers). Further, we successfully prepared graphene film, which has high electrical conductivity (3100.45 S/cm) and thermal conductivity (950.31 W/(m·K)) after graphitization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Liquid-phase exfoliation
Few-layers graphene
Scalable preparation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism for preparing CQDs and graphene

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledged financial support provided by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M672826). In addition, I would like to thank my PhD supervisor professor H. Xu and Y. Lu (Ocean University of China) for their spiritual support and life lessons.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Process flow chart for the preparation of CQDs and graphene; (b) SEM image of CF bundles before electrolysis; (c) SEM image of CF before electrolysis; (d) SEM image of CF after electrolysis; (e) SEM image of EG.
(a) HR-TEM and SEAD image; (b) AFM image; (c) Size distribution; (d) XPS; (e) FTIR; (f) Fluorescence spectra.
(a) SEM images of graphene; (b) AFM images; (c) Size distribution; (d) XPS; (e) Raman; (f) Zeta potential.
(a) Synthesis mechanism of CQDs; (b) Synthesis mechanism of graphene.

(a) Relationship between concentration and viscosity of graphene slurry; (b). SEM of graphene film surface; (c) SEM of cross-section graphene film; (d) Electrical conductivity and thermal diffusivity of graphene film.

head.ce:title: Green, fast and scalable preparation of few-layers graphene

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BTR New Material Group Co., Ltd., Shenzhen 518106, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BTR New Material Group Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BTR New Material Group Co., Ltd., Shenzhen 518106, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Advanced Graphene Technology Co., Ltd, Shenzhen 518106, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Advanced Graphene Technology Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen Advanced Graphene Technology Co., Ltd, Shenzhen 518106, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The novel electrochemical interface technology is like a “pencil sharpening” method to prepare CQDs. At the same time, CQDs are like a “tape” in an aqueous solution to exfoliate graphite layers to produce graphene.

Few-layers graphene is prepared by one-step liquid-phase exfoliation with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as exfoliating agents, which is prepared by a novel electrochemical interface technology. The morphology, structure and composition of CQDs and graphene, as well as the mechanism for the realization of large-scale preparation of CQDs and graphene was proposed. The results showed that CQDs with D50 size of 55.12 nm has excellent dispersion and exfoliation properties for graphite, and the graphene yield is as high as 97.25% (D50 size of 3.651 μm and 1–5 layers). Further, we successfully prepared graphene film, which has high electrical conductivity (3100.45 S/cm) and thermal conductivity (950.31 W/(m·K)) after graphitization.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene has attracted extensive attention because of its excellent physical and chemical properties, such as large specific surface area, high electronic conductivity and high thermal conductivity. Preparation methods of graphene powders mainly include micro-mechanical exfoliation method, liquid-phase exfoliation method (LPE), chemical redox method and electrochemical method [1,2]. The LPE has the advantages of simple preparation process, low cost, green environmental protection, which was the most widely used method in recent years [3,4]. However, duo to the strong van der Waals force between layers, this LPE method of direct graphite peeling is not ideal. In general, organic solvents using N-1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) as stabilizers could disperse the graphite well [5,6], but the exfoliation yield of graphene was only 20%. In addition, organic solvents have a high boiling point, high toxicity and low cost. Therefore, aqueous exfoliating agents with high biocompatibility and easy post-processing have become the mainstream of LPE research. Studies have shown that the surface tension of water is as high as 72.8 mJ/m2, while that of graphene is only 40–50 mJ/m2 [7,8]. Therefore, the liquid-phase exfoliation of graphite in water solvent could only produce graphite fragment. Using conventional aqueous surfactant, such as sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS) and N, N-dimethyl-2,9-diazaperpropyrenium dichloride (DAP) [9,10], the yield is less than 70%. Therefore, the selection of appropriate surfactants is the key to the green-scale preparation of graphene with LPE method.

Graphene derivatives could be regard as new type of carbon-based surfactants, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), CQDs and graphene oxide (GO), because of their lipophilic sp2 carbon rings and hydrophilic functional groups on the surface [11–13]. Xu et al. [14] prepared CQDs as exfoliating agents by hydrothermal method, and prepared graphene with a yield of up to 82%. Tung et al. [15] utilized GO as a stabilizer and exfoliating agents, and the graphene yield with the aid of ultrasound was as high as 40%. Zeng et al. [16] synthesized a series of GQDs modified with different functional groups (sulfonyl, amino and carboxyl), but the graphene yield was less than 20% by ultrasound-assisted method. In summary, green-scale production of few-layers graphene still faces huge challenges, and graphene derivatives as new surfactants are of great significance for the industrial production of graphene.

In order to achieve green, fast and scale preparation of graphene, herein, a novel electrochemical interface technique was established, and one-step synthesis of CQDs from carbon fiber, providing sufficient exfoliating agents for preparing few-layers graphene. Further, few-layers graphene was prepared by one-step liquid-phase exfoliation under high-pressure homogenization, so as to achieve green, fast and scalable preparation of graphene. In addition, the electrical and thermal properties of graphene film were explored, laying a foundation for the application of graphene.

Carbon fibers (CF, 12 K) came from Zhongfu Shenying Carbon Fiber Co., Ltd. Expanded graphite (EG, carbon content 98.0%) powders were purchased from Wulanchabu Dasheng Graphite New Material Co., Ltd. Ammonium sulfate ((NH4)2SO4, AR) came from Sinopharm group. All chemicals were used as receiving without further purification. Deionized water (resistivity ∼18.2 MΩ cm) obtained through a Milli-Q system used throughout all experiments.

Synthesis of CQDs: Under a constant voltage of 15.0 V, the electrochemical exfoliation process was carried out in a two-electrode cell. 70 bundles of CF beams clamped by a self-made Ti clip as working anode, and Ti mesh (40 × 40 mm2, TA2) as cathode in 1.0 mol/L (NH4)2SO4 electrolytes for 10 h. After that, the electrolyte filtered through 0.1 µm polypropylene membrane to remove the insoluble residue, and the filtrate was CQDs solution.

EG (1.0 g) was added to 0.5 L CQDs solution (2.0 g/L), and dispersed under a high-speed shear (linear velocity 30.0 m/s) for 30 min. The dispersion was added to the high-pressure homogenizer and homogenized 1–5 times under the pressure of 300–1800 psi. Then, the dispersion was washed 5 times with deionized water with 0.1 μm polypropylene filter membrane. The aqueous slurry was spray-dried for 24 h to obtain graphene powders, and the washing solution could be used as electrolyte to further prepare CQDs.

The graphene slurry was rolled to form graphene film, and then dried at 353 K to remove the remaining water. Subsequently, the graphene film was rolled at 5.0 MPa and 298 K, and thermal reduction at 400–2800 °C, and further molded for 48 h at 60 MPa to obtain graphene film.

Morphology and distribution of CQDs and graphene were obtained by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), atomic force microscopic (AFM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). HR-TEM images were obtained by a FEI TECNAI G2 F20 transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The test sample was obtained by dip-coated the aqueous dispersion on a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying at room temperature. AFM images were obtained by using a Digital Instrument Nanoscope (Veeco Multimode). The sample was prepared by dip-coated the dispersion on a clean mica wafer and dried at room temperature. The SEM images were recorded using a Hitachi S4800 with an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. The size and zeta potential (ζ) were obtained by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano Zs.

Chemical component and structure of CQDs and graphene were carried out by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman and thermos gravimetric analyzer (TGA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Escalab 250Xi) with an Al Kα as the X-ray source. The sample was prepared by dip-coated the dispersions on a clean silicon wafer and dried at room temperature. FTIR spectrum was measured by using a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer. The Raman spectrum was measured by using a Laser Confocal Micro Raman Spectroscopy (532 nm, Labram Hr800). X-ray diffraction (XRD) of CQDs and graphene film were conducted by a Powder X-Ray Diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) that was operated at 40 kV and 40 mA. The thermogravimetric (TG) curves of the material measured by using TG 209 F3 Tarsus (Netsch Scientific Instruments Trading). The temperature range was 25–800 °C under nitrogen protection, and the heating rate was 10 °C/min. Optical characteristic of CQDs was measured by using fluorescence spectra (FL, FLS980 Series of Fluorescence Spectrometers).

The dynamic viscosity of samples was measured with a Brookfield viscometer. The electrical resistance of the graphene product was measured by using a four-probe tester (FT-341, Ningbo Ruikeweiye Instrument Co., Ltd.). The thickness of graphene films was measured at 3–5 points, and the positive and negative currents were performed during the test, then the mean value was taken. The thermal diffusivity of samples was measured by using a Netzsch LFA 467. The graphene film on a nylon substrate was prepared through roll coating technology. The sample shape was circular, the diameter was 25 mm, thickness was 80 μm, density was 2.008 g/cm3, specific heat capacity was 850 J/(kg·K) and testing temperature was 25 °C.

The specific process flow chart for preparing CQDs and graphene was shown in Fig. 1 (a). The down end-face of CF was kept exact and continuous contact with electrolyte surface by adjusting the vertical height of anodic clamp. The down end-face of CF as shown in Fig. 1(b) and (c) was electrochemically etched at a constant potential of 15.0 V in 1.0 mol/L (NH4)2SO4 electrolytes for 10 h. During the whole process, the electrolysis current of CF electrode was maintained at 4.0–5.5 A (current density: 12.50–17.19 A cm−2), and the color of electrolyte rapidly turned from colorless to golden brown after 50 s, and to brown after 2 min, finally to dark brown after 30 min as shown in Fig. 1(d). After that, the electrolyte was filtered by 0.1 µm millipore membrane to obtain the CQDs. By deducting the mass of residue, the utilization rate of CF for CQDs was calculated to be as high as 92.87%. Further, the EG powders (Fig. 1(e)) were pre-dispersed into the CQDs solution using high-speed shear equipment, and then homogenized under high pressure. Few-layers graphene was obtained through fluid shear, convection and cavitation. The solution after homogenizing was filtered and washed, and the filtered slurry was graphene products, and the filtrate could be circulated as an electrolyte, thereby achieving green, fast and scale preparation of graphene. Further, the effect of homogenization pressure and times was explored as shown in Fig. S1. The D50 size of graphene products gradually decreased with the homogenization pressure from 300 psi to 1800 psi. The D50 sizes of graphene products were 19.77 μm at 300 psi, and 11.26 μm at 1800 psi. The graphene yield gradually improved with the increase of homogenization pressure and times (Fig. S2). When the homogeneous one time is was 300 psi, the graphene yield was 30.34%, and 91.56% at 1500 psi. Furthermore, the graphene yield could reach 97.25% (D50 size of 3.651 μm and 1–5 layers) when the homogeneous times was 5.

The morphology of CQDs was characterized by HR-TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 (a). It can be seen that CQDs were elliptical with clear lattice fringes, and the crystal plane spacing from the SEAD diffraction was 0.25 nm, corresponding to (100) of its graphite crystal planes respectively [17,18]. The thickness of CQDs was characterized by AFM, as shown in Fig. 2(b). It can be seen that CQDs were spherically distributed with a thickness of 3.60–6.90 nm. Particle size of CQDs was as shown in Fig. 2(c), and the D50 size of CQDs were 55.12 nm. Further, element analysis of CQDs by XPS were shown in Fig. 2(d) and Table S1, CQDs contained C, N and O elements, and the total content of O and N atoms was 57.73%. Fig. S3 shows the typical XRD patterns for CQDs, and the prepared CQDs have a broader (002) peak centered at around 25.204°, and the interlayer spacing is 0.353 nm, which is bigger than that of graphite layers (0.334 nm). This results could be attributed to the functional groups introduced in the oxidation and intercalation of CF. The functional groups of CQDs were characterized by FTIR as shown in Fig. 2(e), the CQDs had lots of O and N functional groups, such as NH2, OH, NCO and NO2, thus CQDs had good dispersion stability and modifiability. The ζ value of CQDs aqueous solutions were −34.60 mV, indicating that CQDs had good colloidal stability [19]. The optical properties of CQDs using 365 nm excitation wavelength was shown in Fig. 2(f), CQDs showed blue-green fluorescence. Recently, the N-doped CQDs has been widely studied in the fields of tunable-photoluminescence and fluorescence detection [20–25], cell imaging [26] and tumor therapy [27–28], thus the prepared CQDs with high N doping (28.90 at.%) has a broad research prospect.

The morphology of graphene products was characterized by SEM, as shown in Fig. 3 (a), the graphene products had a semi-transparent and layered structure, which is a typical morphology feature of graphene [29]. The thickness of graphene products characterized by AFM was shown in Fig. 3(b). It can be seen that the thickness of graphene was 0.3–1.2 nm (1–3 layers). Furthermore, the graphene had large numbers of dot-like substances, which were similar to the used CQDs. Previous studies have shown that GQDs were easily adsorbed on the surface of graphene by π bonds [30], while CQDs had analogous structures to GQDs, which made them easy to form composite materials (CQDs/graphene). Particle size of graphene products was analyzed by laser particle size analyzer as shown in Fig. 3(c), the D50 size of graphene products was 3.651 μm.

Elemental analysis of graphene products by XPS was indicated in Fig. 3(d) and Table S2. The N content of graphene was 1.86 at.%, further indicating that the prepared graphene still adsorbed CQDs. Simultaneously, the high O content (8.17 at.%) also indicated that presence of CQDs on the surface of the prepared graphene, which was consistent with the above results. Raman spectroscopy has unique advantages in characterizing the structure of graphene. The D peak at 1350 cm−1 indicated the structural defects caused by sp3 hybridization, the G peak at 1580 cm−1 indicated the tensile mode of sp2 hybrid carbon, and the intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the D peak to the G peak was used to evaluate the defect degree of carbon materials [31,32]. As shown in Fig. 3(e), the ID/IG intensity of graphene was very weak, being 0.309, indicating that the prepared graphene had fewer structural defects. Combined with the above AFM, FTIR and XPS results, it was effectively proved that the structural defects of graphene products derived from the CQDs. The 2D peak (2700 cm−1) of Raman spectrum and its frequency shift degree represented the stacking pattern and number of graphite layers [33,34]. The 2D peak position of graphene (2690 cm−1) had a significant red shift, which was an important indicator of the conversion of graphite to graphene [35]. In summary, CQDs as exfoliating agents were adsorbed on the graphene surface through π bonds, and cannot be separated by multiple washing, forming new composite materials (CQDs/graphene) between the exfoliating agents and graphene.

The adsorption of exfoliating agents on the graphene surface was quantitatively studied and the mass of the composites after each washing was measured by vacuum drying. The experimental results were shown in Fig. S4, the adsorption mass of graphene gradually decreased with the washing cycles increased, and the adsorption mass remained constant at 4–5 times. The final mass of CQDs adsorbed on the graphene surface (1.0 g) was 88 mg, which was consistent with the above results of AFM, XPS and Raman. The thermal stability and composition of graphene products studied by TG method were shown in Fig. S5. It can be analyzed that the bound water decreased gradually at 25–234 °C, and the active functional groups were lost at 234–514 °C. The remaining mass of graphene products was 93.19 wt% at 514–700 °C. The weight loss rate of graphene products was significantly reduced at 700–800 °C, because the zero-dimensional carbon structure of CQDs had higher activity, and the internal carbon skeleton was easily decomposed at high temperature.

The dispersion stability of graphene products was further studied. It can be seen that graphene could be stably dispersed without delamination after 72 h. The ζ value of graphene products in aqueous solution was shown in Fig. 3(f), which were −39.471 mV, indicating that the aqueous solution of graphene was in a stable colloidal state [36,37]. Combined with the above experimental results, it was shown that the free nano-sized CQDs were easily washed away by deionized water, and the adsorbed CQDs could effectively disperse graphene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for preparing CQDs and graphene
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above experimental results, the synthesis mechanism of CQDs and graphene is discussed in Fig. 4 . In view of the preparation of CQDs, CF has a ribbon-type turbostratic stacking structure by sp2 carbon only in two-dimensional order, and the nano-crystalline graphitic structure has an advantage of being intercalated and stripped off to obtain the CQDs in Fig. 4(a). More importantly, the electrochemical interface technique is the key to achieving high yield of batch stable CQDs. The current density distribution is very uniform on the end-face of CF, thereby promoting the controllable oxidation and intercalation of O· and SO4 2−, and finally, the particle size of CQDs is very uniform (D50 = 55.12 nm), and the CQDs yield is as high as 92.87%. While the traditional electrochemical method randomly oxidizes the immersed electrode in Fig. S6, and produces uneven current density, brings about the irregular particles or even early destruction consequently, and reduces the yield of CQDs.

Further, when the EG powders dispersed into the CQDs solution through high shear equipment, the CQDs were adsorbed to the EG surface through the π bond force, and bound to water through the H bond force to promote the stable dispersion of EG into the aqueous solution (Fig. 4(b)). High-pressure homogenization promoted the fluid to form physical effects such as intense convective shear and cavitation impact, so that EG was stripped into few-layers graphene [38,39]. Yi et al. [40] systematically studied the properties of high-pressure microchannel fluids through fluid dynamics simulation calculations, and found that the exfoliation mechanism of LPE was explained by the combined effect of Reynolds shear stress, high-pressure cavitation, and the mutual collision between graphite flakes. CQDs were adsorbed on the EG surface, and left with graphene together, which could effectively protect the intrinsic structure of graphene from oxidation or damage. At the same time, the surface of graphene was negatively charged by CQDs, which enabled the graphene products to disperse stably in aqueous solution. CQDs were similar to “tape” [30], which were adsorbed on the surface of EG through π bonds interaction, and continuously stripped EG layers under physical force to realize continuous preparation of graphene.

The graphene slurry were adjusted to different concentrations, and their viscosity were measured as shown in Fig. 5 (a) and Fig. S7. It can be seen that the viscosity of graphene slurry gradually improved with the increase of the concentrations, and a lower viscosity of 1547 mPa·s at 4.98 wt%. The nano-sized CQDs had relatively weak π-bonds with graphene and small spatial site resistance, where the CQDs has a spherical structure, similar to a “pulley” structure. The spherical CQDs were favorable to improve the interlayer slip of graphene layers, which resulted in a lower viscosity of graphene slurry, as shown schematically inset image of Fig. 5(a). Adjusting the viscosity of graphene products as shown inset photo of Fig. 5(a), the roll-coating process revealed that the aqueous slurry of graphene was likely to bond to the substrate and form graphene film, as shown in Fig. S8. The microstructure of graphene film was observed using SEM, as shown in Fig. 5(b) and (c). It can be seen from the SEM of the graphene film surface that the graphene sheets have large numbers of wrinkles and their adjacent layers were tightly linked, and the graphene layers were stacked in an orderly manner from the SEM of cross-section graphene film in Fig. 5(c). From the above experimental results, it can be concluded that nano-size CQDs had large surface area and more high-content functional group sites, similar to “adhesive”, which could connect individual graphene sheets with each other and thus had good film-forming properties.

The graphene films were further thermally reduced under argon conditions at 400–2800 °C, and the electrical and thermal properties were shown in Fig. 5(d). It can be seen that the electrical conductivity and thermal diffusivity of the graphene film gradually improved with the increase of reduction temperature, and the data were shown in Table S3. The functional groups of CQDs gradually decreased, and the carbon content improved with the increase of reduction temperature, which greatly accelerated the electron mobility and thus the electrical conductivity of graphene films. When the reduction temperature reached 2800 °C, the carbon content of the graphene film was as high as 100% (Fig. S9), and the electrical conductivity of graphene film were 3100.45 S/cm. Meanwhile, it also can be seen that the thermal diffusivity of graphene film improved with the increase of reduction temperature, and a thermal diffusivity of 556.78 mm2/s (Thermal conductivity was 950.31 W/(m·K) at 25 °C) at 2800℃. Fig. S10 shows the typical XRD patterns for graphene film. After graphitization at 2800 °C, the (002) diffraction peak of the graphene film located at 26.52°, close to that of single crystals of graphite (26.58°), indicating that the graphene film formed a highly ordered graphite structure after graphitization. Fig. S11 shows the Raman spectrum of graphene film. It can be seen that the graphene film after graphitization has no D peak, indicating that the prepared graphene film has complete crystal structure and few defects, which is consistent with the results of XRD patterns. Combined with the TG analysis of graphene, when the reduction temperature was higher than 700 °C, increasing the temperature was favorable to remove the functional groups of CQDs, which was favorable to the phonon transport, thus improving the thermal diffusivity. Simultaneously, nano-sized CQDs adsorbed on graphene were beneficial to reduce the interfacial contact between graphene sheets, thus reducing the mean free range of phonon and improving the thermal diffusivity of graphene films.

A novel electrochemical interface technology was used to realize the large-scale preparation of CQDs, and further use it as a stripping agent to realize green, fast and scalable preparation of few-layers graphene. As a new type of nano-carbon surfactants, CQDs with D50 size of 55.12 nm has excellent dispersion and exfoliation properties for graphite. The D50 size of graphene products was 3.651 μm, the thickness was 1–5 layers, and the yield was as high as 97.25%. Further, the prepared graphene film has high electrical conductivity (3100.45 S/cm) and thermal conductivity (950.31 W/(m·K)) after graphitization, laying a foundation for the application of graphene.

Quanzhu Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Guohui Yuan: Validation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Kejun Guo: Resources, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Shaojun Li: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Minjing Lin: Resources, Data curation. Jing Hong: Resources, Data curation. Youyuan Huang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledged financial support provided by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M672826). In addition, I would like to thank my PhD supervisor professor H. Xu and Y. Lu (Ocean University of China) for their spiritual support and life lessons.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2021.100303.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Top-down synthesis of graphene: a comprehensive review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kumar N., Salehiyan R., Chauke V., Joseph Botlhoko O., Setshedi K., Scriba M., Masukume M., Sinha Ray S. Top-down synthesis of graphene: A comprehensive review. FlatChem 27 (2021) 100224. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2021.100224

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene based emergent nanolights: a short review on the synthesis, properties and application

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanisms of liquid-phase exfoliation for the production of graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li Z., Young R.J., Backes C., Zhao W., Zhang X., Zhukov A.A., Tillotson E., Conlan A.P., Ding F., Haigh S.J., Novoselov K.S., Coleman J.N., Mechanisms of liquid-phase exfoliation for the production of graphene. ACS Nano 14 (2020) 10976-10985. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsnano.0c03916

Kauling A.P., Seefeldt A.T., Pisoni D.P., Pradeep R.C., Bentini R., Oliveira R.V.B., Novoselov K.S., Castro Neto A.H., The worldwide graphene flake production. Adv. Mater. 30 (2018) 1803784. https://doi.org/10.1002/adma.201803784

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvent-exfoliated graphene at extremely high concentration

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Khan U., Porwal H., O’Neill A., Nawaz K., May P., Coleman J.N., Solvent-exfoliated graphene at extremely high concentration. Langmuir 27 (2011) 9077-9082. https://doi.org/10.1021/la201797h

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cationic surfactant mediated exfoliation of graphite into graphene flakes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Vadukumpully S., Paul J., Valiyaveettil S., Cationic surfactant mediated exfoliation of graphite into graphene flakes. Carbon 47 (2009) 3288-3294. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2009.07.049

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of graphene dispersion and interfacial bonding on the mechanical properties of metal matrix composites: an overview

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ghodrati H., Ghomashchi R., Effect of graphene dispersion and interfacial bonding on the mechanical properties of metal matrix composites: An overview. FlatChem 16 (2019) 100113. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2019.100113

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surfactant mediated liquid phase exfoliation of graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Narayan R., Kim S.O., Surfactant mediated liquid phase exfoliation of graphene. Nano Convergence 2 (2015) 20. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40580-015-0050-x

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Localized in-situ polymerization on graphene surfaces for stabilized graphene dispersions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Das S., Wajid A.S., Shelburne J.L., Liao Y.C., Green M.J., Localized in-situ polymerization on graphene surfaces for stabilized graphene dispersions. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 3 (2011) 1844-1851. https://doi.org/10.1021/am1011436

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of deoxy group on the high concentration of graphene in surfactant/water media

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ramalingam P., Pusuluri S.T., Periasamy S., Veerabahu R., Kulandaivel J., Role of deoxy group on the high concentration of graphene in surfactant/water media. RSC Adv. 3 (2013) 2369-2378. https://doi.org/10.1039/C2RA22343K

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Insights into the oxidation mechanism of sp2-sp3 hybrid carbon materials: preparation of a water-soluble 2D porous conductive network and detectable molecule separation
